paper_title: \bf From Gödel incompleteness to the consistency of circuit lower bounds %Circuit lower bounds for exponential time are consistent with $\S^1_2$

perturbations.original:
\begin{theorem}[Takeuti] \label{thm:takeuti} There is a bounded formula which is provable in $\V^1_2$ but not in~$\S^1_2$.
\end{theorem}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{theorem}[Takeuti] \label{thm:takeuti} There is a bounded formula which is provable in $\S^1_2$ but not in~$\V^1_2$.
\end{theorem}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation reverses the direction of the separation. The paper states that $\V^1_2$ is a stronger theory that extends $\T_2$ (and thus $\S^1_2$), so a formula provable in the weaker $\S^1_2$ must be provable in the stronger $\V^1_2$. Takeuti's result is that the stronger theory proves something the weaker one does not.

The perturbation removes the $\forall\Sigma^{1,b}_1$ prefix from the theories $\V^1_2$ and $\U^1_2$. The magnification argument specifically relates the provability of number-sort (first-order) consequences. Without the restriction to the number-sort fragment, the claim is flawed because $\V^1_2$ and $\U^1_2$ are second-order theories, and the reduction in magnification arguments typically targets the first-order strength.

perturbations.original:
\begin{lemma}\label{lem:circuit} For every quantifier-free $\PV$-formula $F(\bar x)$ there is $c\in\N$ such that
$$
\S^1_2\vdash\forall n{\in}\Log\ \exists C{<}2^{n^c}\ \forall \bar x{<}2^n\ (C(\bar x)=1 \leftrightarrow F(\bar x)).
$$
\end{lemma}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{lemma}\label{lem:circuit} For every quantifier-free $\PV$-formula $F(\bar x)$ there is $c\in\N$ such that
$$
\S^1_2\vdash\forall n{\in}\N\ \exists C{<}2^{n^c}\ \forall \bar x{<}2^n\ (C(\bar x)=1 \leftrightarrow F(\bar x)).
$$
\end{lemma}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation changes the quantifier from 'n in Log' to 'n in N'. In S1_2, the existence of circuits (which are objects of size 2^(n^c)) can only be proven for 'small' n (i.e., n in Log), because the theory cannot prove the existence of exponentially large objects for all n in N.

The perturbation swaps the roles of the theories S1_2 and U1_2. In bounded arithmetic, U1_2 is a stronger theory than S1_2; therefore, the implication from the general formula to the restricted (strict) formula should be provable in the stronger theory U1_2, while the reverse implication (that the strict version implies the general one) is provable in the weaker theory S1_2.

The lemma claims that a model of S^i_2 (which satisfies length induction) can be expanded to a model of T^i_2(alpha) (which satisfies full induction). This is a known non-trivial jump in strength; S^i_2 is generally not known to prove the induction axioms required for T^i_2, especially in the relativized setting with the circuit class C_N.

The perturbation swaps the bounds and the theories. S1_2 is only capable of proving properties about computations with logarithmic space bounds (or length-bounded parameters like |s|) because it relies on length induction, whereas T1_2 can handle polynomial space bounds (s) using full induction.

Replacing s with 2^s in S1_2 is incorrect because S1_2 is a theory of bounded arithmetic where quantifiers are bounded by polynomials. A bound of 2^s would exceed the growth rate of the smash function (#) and the induction capabilities of S1_2, which typically handles length-bounded induction (logarithmic in the value).

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition} Let~$M$ be an oracle machine with~$k \in \N$ input tapes and~$\ell \in \N$
  oracle tapes. The machine~$M$ is {\em explicit} if there are
  terms~$t_0(\bar x),s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x)$ such that
\begin{eqnarray*}
\S^1_2(\alpha)&\vdash& \q{$Y$ is a partial time-$t$ space-$s$ query-$q$ computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar{x}$}\\
&&\ \to \textit{time}^Y(t)<t_0(\bar x)\wedge \textit{space}^Y(t)<s_0(\bar x)\wedge \textit{query}^{Y}(t)<|q_0(\bar x)|.
\end{eqnarray*}
In this case we say that $M$ is {\em witnessed} by $t_0,s_0,q_0$ and write 
\begin{eqnarray*}
\Bd^0_M(\bar x)&:=&\Bd^0(M,\bar x,s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x))\\ 
\Bd_M(\bar x)&:=&\Bd(M,\bar x,t_0(\bar x),s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x)).
\end{eqnarray*}
The machine~$M$ is an {\em explicit~$\EXP$-machine} if there exists a
term~$t_M(x)$ such that the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$
and~$s_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$.  
The machine~$M$
is an {\em explicit~$\PSPACE$-machine} if there exists a term~$t_M(x)$
such that the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$ and~$s_0(\bar
x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$. 
The machine~$M$ is an
{\em explicit~$\P$-machine} if there exists a term~$t_M(x)$ such that
the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$ and~$s_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar
x)|$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$.  In all three cases we say that~$M$ is \emph{witnessed} by the term~$t_M(\bar x)$.
%
% Saying that $M$ is an {\em explicit $\EXP$-, $\PSPACE$- or $\P$-machine} 
% means that so it is witnessed by some term $t_M(\bar x)$. 
%
%Given such an $M$, the formula \q{$Y$ is $t_M(\bar x)$-bounded} is $$Y{<}\Bd(M,t,s,q,\bar x)\wedge\forall u{\le} t\ Y_u{<}\Bd_0(M,s,q,\bar x)$$ for the respective values of $t,s,q$. 
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition} Let~$M$ be an oracle machine with~$k \in \N$ input tapes and~$\ell \in \N$
  oracle tapes. The machine~$M$ is {\em explicit} if there are
  terms~$t_0(\bar x),s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x)$ such that
\begin{eqnarray*}
\S^1_2(\alpha)&\vdash& \q{$Y$ is a partial time-$t$ space-$s$ query-$q$ computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar{x}$}\\
&&\ \to \textit{time}^Y(t)<t_0(\bar x)\wedge \textit{space}^Y(t)<s_0(\bar x)\wedge \textit{query}^{Y}(t)<q_0(\bar x).
\end{eqnarray*}
In this case we say that $M$ is {\em witnessed} by $t_0,s_0,q_0$ and write 
\begin{eqnarray*}
\Bd^0_M(\bar x)&:=&\Bd^0(M,\bar x,s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x))\\ 
\Bd_M(\bar x)&:=&\Bd(M,\bar x,t_0(\bar x),s_0(\bar x),q_0(\bar x)).
\end{eqnarray*}
The machine~$M$ is an {\em explicit~$\EXP$-machine} if there exists a
term~$t_M(x)$ such that the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$
and~$s_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$.  
The machine~$M$
is an {\em explicit~$\PSPACE$-machine} if there exists a term~$t_M(x)$
such that the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$ and~$s_0(\bar
x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$. 
The machine~$M$ is an
{\em explicit~$\P$-machine} if there exists a term~$t_M(x)$ such that
the above holds for~$t_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$ and~$s_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar
x)|$, and~$q_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$.  In all three cases we say that~$M$ is \emph{witnessed} by the term~$t_M(\bar x).
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation changes the query bound for an explicit P-machine from $q_0(\bar x)=t_M(\bar x)$ to $q_0(\bar x)=|t_M(\bar x)|$. In the context of bounded arithmetic, the number of queries for a polynomial-time machine should be bounded by a term (polynomial in the length of the input), not the length of a term (logarithmic in the length of the input), which would incorrectly restrict the machine to a sub-class of P.

The perturbation changes the definition of $\meyer^c_M$ from requiring $C_x$ to be a 'halting' computation to an 'accepting' computation. This is incorrect because the $\meyer$ formula is intended to formalize that a circuit can simulate the machine's behavior (halting with a result), whereas requiring an 'accepting' computation for every $x$ would imply the machine accepts every input, which is not the intended meaning of circuit simulation for a general machine $M$.

The perturbation changes the theory in parts (c) and (d) from $\T^1_2(\alpha)$ to $\S^1_2(\alpha)$. In bounded arithmetic, $\S^1_2$ is generally too weak to prove the equivalence between different formalizations of complexity statements (like the equivalence between having a circuit and having an accepting computation witness), which typically requires the stronger induction available in $\T^1_2$.

The perturbation changes the theory in part (d) from T^1_2(alpha) to S^1_2(alpha). The context explicitly states that 'only the last implication seems to need T^1_2(alpha)', making the use of S^1_2(alpha) in (d) a contradiction of the paper's own claim.

The theorem is intended to show standard circuit lower bounds (not-subset-of Ppoly). Changing the conclusion to almost-everywhere lower bounds (not-subset-of ioPpoly) here is premature and incorrect, as the paper later introduces Theorem 15 specifically to handle the 'io' (almost-everywhere) case as a generalization.

perturbations.original:
\begin{theorem}\label{thm:wit} 
Let $\varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$ be a $\hat\Sigma^{1,b}_1$-formula.
\begin{enumerate}\itemsep=0pt
\item[(a)] If $\V^1_2\vdash \varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$, then there is an explicit oracle
$\EXP$-machine $M$ such that
$$
\S^1_2(\alpha)\vdash \q{$Y$ is a halting computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar x$} \to \varphi(\bar X,\bar x).
$$
\item[(b)] If $\U^1_2\vdash \varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$, then there is an explicit oracle
$\PSPACE$-machine $M$ such that
$$
\S^1_2(\alpha)\vdash \q{$Y$ is a halting computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar x$} \to \varphi(\bar X,\bar x).
$$ 
\end{enumerate}
\end{theorem}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{theorem}\label{thm:wit} 
Let $\varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$ be a $\hat\Sigma^{1,b}_1$-formula.
\begin{enumerate}\itemsep=0pt
\item[(a)] If $\V^1_2\vdash \varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$, then there is an explicit oracle
$\PSPACE$-machine $M$ such that
$$
\S^1_2(\alpha)\vdash \q{$Y$ is a halting computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar x$} \to \varphi(\bar X,\bar x).
$$
\item[(b)] If $\U^1_2\vdash \varphi(\bar X,\bar x)$, then there is an explicit oracle
$\EXP$-machine $M$ such that
$$
\S^1_2(\alpha)\vdash \q{$Y$ is a halting computation of $M^{\bar X}$ on $\bar x$} \to \varphi(\bar X,\bar x).
$$ 
\end{enumerate}
\end{theorem}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation swaps the complexity of the witnessing machines. In bounded arithmetic, V^1_2 is associated with EXP-computable functions and U^1_2 with PSPACE-computable functions. Swapping them violates the standard witnessing correspondence used throughout the paper.

The corollary should use the 'meyer' formulas (gamma-formulas) to enable the simulation of stronger theories in weaker ones. Replacing 'meyer' with 'alpha' (the standard lower bound formula) is incorrect because 'alpha' does not provide the same simulation strength required for the proof of consistency.

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition} Let~$c\in\N$ and let~$M$ be an explicit~$\EXP$-
  or~$\PSPACE$-machine~$M$. Define
$$
\io\meyer^{c}_{M}:=\exists^{\infty}n{\in}\Lg\ \exists C{<}2^{n^c}\ \forall x{<}2^n\ \q{$C_x(\cdot)$ is a halting computation of $M$ on $x$}.
$$
Set
$
\q{$\EXP\not\subseteq\ioPpoly$}:=\big\{\neg \io\meyer^{c}_{M_1}\mid c\in\N \big\}
$ and $
\q{$\PSPACE\not\subseteq\ioPpoly$}:=\big\{\neg \io\meyer^{c}_{M_2}\mid c\in\N \big\}.$
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition} Let~$c\in\N$ and let~$M$ be an explicit~$\EXP$-
  or~$\PSPACE$-machine~$M$. Define
$$
\io\meyer^{c}_{M}:=\forall u\ \exists n{\in}\Lg\ (|n|>|u| \wedge \exists C{<}2^{n^c}\ \forall x{<}2^n\ \q{$C_x(\cdot)$ is a halting computation of $M$ on $x$}).
$$
Set
$
\q{$\EXP\not\subseteq\ioPpoly$}:=\big\{\neg \io\meyer^{c}_{M_1}\mid c\in\N \big\}
$ and $
\q{$\PSPACE\not\subseteq\ioPpoly$}:=\big\{\neg \io\meyer^{c}_{M_2}\mid c\in\N \big\}.$
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation changes the quantifier in the definition of $\exists^{\infty}n{\in}\Lg$ from $|v|>|u|$ to $|n|>|u|$. In the context of the paper's notation for 'infinitely many', $n$ is the value (the length), and the condition should be on the value $n$ being larger than the length of some $u$, or $n$ being the length of some $v$ where $|v|>|u|$. The original text defines $\exists^{\infty}n{\in}\Lg$ as $\forall u \exists v (|v|>|u| \wedge \dots)$, meaning $n$ is substituted by $|v|$. The perturbation introduces a type mismatch between the variable $n$ and the length $|n|$.

The perturbation changes the conclusion from a lower bound (not-subset-of) to an upper bound (subset-of). This contradicts the entire purpose of the paper, which is to prove the consistency of circuit lower bounds.
